description: Evaluations for all 25 GRC skills: ISO 27001, SOC 2, FedRAMP, GDPR, HIPAA, NIST CSF, PCI DSS, TSA Cybersecurity, ISO 42001, ISO 27701, DORA, DPDPA, CMMC 2.0, NIST AI RMF, SWIFT CSP, ISM, NIS2, CCPA/CPRA, CSRD, CIS Controls v8, EAR, NIST SP 800-53, EU AI Act

evals.prompt: We are a 120-person SaaS company that has never had any formal information security certification. We store customer PII and financial data in AWS. Our CEO wants ISO 27001 certification within 18 months. Give us a realistic gap assessment of what we are likely missing and a phased roadmap to achieve certification.
evals.expected_output: A structured gap assessment covering likely missing elements (ISMS policy, risk assessment process, asset inventory, access control procedures, incident response, supplier management, internal audit, management review) plus a phased 18-month roadmap with clear milestones. Should reference ISO 27001:2022 clauses and Annex A controls.
assertions: References ISO 27001:2022 (not just 2013) and mentions Annex A controls
Identifies at least 5 distinct gap areas (e.g., risk assessment, asset management, incident response, access control, supplier management)
Provides a phased roadmap with at least 3 phases or time-based milestones
Mentions the mandatory clauses 4-10 and their requirements
Includes practical action items, not just abstract principles

evals.prompt: Draft an Information Security Policy for our company. We are a fintech startup with 80 employees. Keep it concise and suitable for ISO 27001 certification.
evals.expected_output: A complete Information Security Policy document with sections covering scope, objectives, security principles, roles and responsibilities, compliance requirements, and review cycle. Should be suitable for clause 5.2 of ISO 27001:2022.
assertions: Policy document has a clear title, scope, and purpose section
Mentions top management commitment (aligns with clause 5.1)
Includes roles and responsibilities section
States review frequency (at least annually)
References or aligns with ISO 27001:2022 requirements

evals.prompt: What are the key differences between ISO 27001:2013 and ISO 27001:2022? We are currently certified under the 2013 version and need to transition. What controls were added, changed, or removed?
evals.expected_output: Detailed comparison covering: 11 new controls added in 2022, restructured Annex A from 14 domains/114 controls to 4 themes/93 controls, new control categories (organizational, people, physical, technological), notable new controls like threat intelligence, cloud security, data masking, secure coding. Transition deadline and process.
assertions: States the number of controls changed (93 in 2022 vs 114 in 2013)
Mentions the 4 new control themes/categories in 2022 (organizational, people, physical, technological)
Lists at least 3 specific new controls added in 2022 (e.g., threat intelligence, cloud security, data masking)
Mentions transition deadline or certification validity period
Explains the Annex A restructuring from 14 domains to 4 themes

evals.prompt: Explain how to conduct an ISO 27001 risk assessment. We have no prior experience with formal risk assessments. Walk us through the methodology, what we need to document, and how to determine which risks need treatment.
evals.expected_output: Step-by-step risk assessment methodology covering: defining scope and criteria, asset identification, threat and vulnerability identification, likelihood and impact scoring, risk evaluation against acceptance criteria, risk treatment options (accept, avoid, transfer, mitigate), Statement of Applicability (SoA), and documentation requirements.
assertions: Explains the four risk treatment options (accept, avoid, transfer, mitigate)
Mentions the Statement of Applicability (SoA) as a required output
Describes risk scoring/evaluation methodology (likelihood + impact)
References clause 6.1 (risk assessment) of ISO 27001
Explains risk acceptance criteria concept

evals.prompt: We received a major nonconformity during our ISO 27001 audit for 'insufficient evidence of management review.' What exactly does management review require, what evidence should we have documented, and how do we write a corrective action plan to close this nonconformity?
evals.expected_output: Detailed guidance on clause 9.3 management review requirements (inputs, outputs, frequency, participants), what evidence/records are required, and a corrective action plan template with root cause analysis, immediate correction, and preventive measures.
assertions: References clause 9.3 of ISO 27001 for management review
Lists required management review inputs (e.g., audit results, risk assessment status, security incidents, performance metrics)
Lists required management review outputs (e.g., decisions on ISMS improvement, resource needs)
Provides corrective action plan structure with root cause analysis
Mentions evidence/records that satisfy the requirement (meeting minutes, attendance, decisions)

evals.prompt: We are a B2B SaaS startup and our first enterprise customer is asking for a SOC 2 Type 2 report. We have never done any SOC 2 work. Explain the difference between Type 1 and Type 2, which one we should pursue, and what the typical timeline looks like.
evals.expected_output: Clear explanation of Type 1 (point-in-time design effectiveness) vs Type 2 (6-12 month operating effectiveness), recommendation to pursue Type 1 first then Type 2, typical timeline (3-6 months for Type 1 readiness, 6-12 month observation period for Type 2), and guidance on selecting a CPA auditor.
assertions: Clearly distinguishes Type 1 (design effectiveness at point in time) from Type 2 (operating effectiveness over period)
Mentions the typical observation period for Type 2 (at least 6 months)
Recommends or explains the option to start with Type 1
Mentions the 5 Trust Services Criteria categories
Addresses the need for a licensed CPA firm to conduct the audit

evals.prompt: List the key controls we need for SOC 2 Security (CC) criteria. We are a cloud-native company using AWS, GitHub, and Okta. Give us a practical control checklist.
evals.expected_output: Comprehensive checklist of CC controls covering: CC1 (control environment), CC2 (communication and information), CC3 (risk assessment), CC4 (monitoring), CC5 (control activities), CC6 (logical access), CC7 (system operations), CC8 (change management), CC9 (risk mitigation). Should be practical and AWS/Okta specific.
assertions: Covers logical and physical access controls (CC6 series)
Mentions change management controls (CC8)
Addresses incident response and monitoring (CC7)
Includes vendor/third-party risk management (CC9)
References at least 5 of the CC control category series (CC1-CC9)

evals.prompt: A customer wants us to include the Availability criteria in our SOC 2 report in addition to Security. What additional controls and commitments does this require? How does it differ from just having Security?
evals.expected_output: Explanation of Availability criteria (A series controls), including uptime commitments, disaster recovery, backup procedures, performance monitoring, incident response for availability, and how to document system availability commitments in the system description.
assertions: Identifies Availability as one of the 5 Trust Services Criteria
Mentions uptime/availability commitments and SLAs
Covers disaster recovery and business continuity requirements
Explains backup and recovery testing requirements
Distinguishes A-series controls from CC-series controls

evals.prompt: Write a sample Access Control Policy suitable for SOC 2 Type 2 audit evidence. We are a 50-person SaaS company using Okta for SSO.
evals.expected_output: Complete Access Control Policy document covering: purpose, scope, access provisioning/deprovisioning, least privilege principle, privileged access management, MFA requirements, access reviews, guest/contractor access, and review cycle.
assertions: Includes access provisioning and deprovisioning procedures
Addresses least privilege and need-to-know principles
Mentions MFA requirements
Includes periodic access review requirements
Has clear scope, purpose, and review/approval sections

evals.prompt: Our SOC 2 audit found an exception: we had a former employee whose access was not revoked for 45 days after termination. How serious is this? What control failures does it represent and how should we write the management response?
evals.expected_output: Assessment of severity (moderately serious for Type 2), identification of control failures (CC6.2 logical access provisioning, HR offboarding process gap), and a professional management response that acknowledges the exception, explains root cause, and describes remediation steps.
assertions: References the relevant CC control (CC6.2 or CC6.1 relating to logical access termination)
Assesses the risk/severity of the exception accurately
Provides a management response with root cause analysis
Describes specific remediation steps (e.g., automated deprovisioning, HRIS integration)
Explains impact on the overall audit opinion

evals.prompt: We are a cloud software vendor interested in selling to US federal agencies. Explain the FedRAMP authorization process from start to finish, the difference between Agency ATO and JAB P-ATO, and which path we should consider for a first authorization.
evals.expected_output: Complete FedRAMP authorization pathway explanation covering: FedRAMP Ready, Authorized phases, Agency ATO (sponsor-driven, faster) vs JAB P-ATO (Joint Authorization Board, higher bar but broader acceptance), 3PAO assessment, System Security Plan, SAP, SAR, POA&M, and continuous monitoring requirements.
assertions: Explains the difference between Agency ATO and JAB P-ATO
Mentions the role of a 3PAO (Third Party Assessment Organization)
Lists the core FedRAMP documentation: SSP, SAP, SAR, POA&M
Explains FedRAMP Ready designation as a precursor step
Addresses continuous monitoring (ConMon) requirements post-authorization

evals.prompt: What is the difference between FedRAMP Low, Moderate, and High impact levels? Our product handles law enforcement sensitive data. Which impact level applies to us?
evals.expected_output: Clear explanation of the three impact levels based on FIPS 199 categorization, data sensitivity criteria for each, typical use cases, and guidance that law enforcement sensitive data likely falls under High impact with specific control requirements.
assertions: Explains all three impact levels: Low, Moderate, High
References FIPS 199 as the basis for impact categorization
States approximate number of controls for each level
Addresses law enforcement data sensitivity and likely High impact designation
Mentions LI-SaaS as a special category for Low impact

evals.prompt: What is FedRAMP 20x and how does it change the authorization process? Should we plan for FedRAMP 20x or pursue traditional FedRAMP authorization?
evals.expected_output: Explanation of FedRAMP 20x as the modernization initiative, key changes (machine-readable documentation, automated continuous monitoring, streamlined assessment), current status and timeline, and practical advice on whether to pursue traditional or 20x path.
assertions: Explains FedRAMP 20x as a modernization/automation-focused initiative
Mentions key changes: automation, machine-readable controls (OSCAL), continuous monitoring improvements
Addresses the current status and timeline of 20x rollout
Provides practical guidance on path selection
Mentions OSCAL (Open Security Controls Assessment Language)

evals.prompt: Draft a System Boundary Description for a FedRAMP Moderate SSP. Our system is a web application hosted on AWS GovCloud with a PostgreSQL database, Redis cache, and integrations with two external SaaS tools (Okta for authentication and SendGrid for email).
evals.expected_output: A complete system boundary description covering: authorization boundary definition, included components (web app, database, cache, AWS GovCloud services), external service dependencies and their FedRAMP authorization status, data flows, interconnections documentation, and exclusions.
assertions: Defines the authorization boundary clearly
Lists all included system components (web app, PostgreSQL, Redis, AWS GovCloud)
Addresses external SaaS integrations (Okta, SendGrid) and their FedRAMP status
Mentions interconnection security agreements (ISA/MOU) for external systems
Notes AWS GovCloud as the infrastructure layer and its FedRAMP inheritance

evals.prompt: We received a POA&M item for 'Vulnerability scan findings not remediated within required timeframes for Critical vulnerabilities.' What are the FedRAMP remediation timeframes for Critical, High, and Medium vulnerabilities, and how do we write a proper POA&M entry?
evals.expected_output: FedRAMP vulnerability remediation timeframes (Critical: 15 days, High: 30 days, Moderate: 90 days, Low: 180 days), explanation of POA&M structure (weakness description, point of contact, resources required, scheduled completion date, milestones), and a sample POA&M entry.
assertions: States correct FedRAMP remediation timeframe for Critical vulnerabilities (15 days)
States correct timeframe for High vulnerabilities (30 days)
Explains the required POA&M fields and structure
Provides or describes a sample POA&M entry
Mentions that deviation from timeframes requires a deviation request

evals.prompt: We are a US-based SaaS company that has just signed our first European customer. What are the key GDPR obligations we need to comply with? We process employee data, customer account data, and usage analytics. Give us a compliance checklist.
evals.expected_output: Comprehensive GDPR compliance checklist covering: lawful basis for processing, privacy notices, data subject rights procedures, data processing agreements, international transfer mechanisms, breach notification, DPO assessment, and data retention policies.
assertions: Covers the 6 lawful bases for processing under Article 6
Mentions privacy notice requirements (Articles 13/14)
Addresses data subject rights (Articles 15-22)
Covers international transfer mechanisms (SCCs, adequacy decisions)
Mentions breach notification requirements (72-hour timeline under Article 33)

evals.prompt: Draft a GDPR-compliant Data Processing Agreement (DPA) for use with our SaaS customers where we act as a data processor. Include all required clauses under Article 28.
evals.expected_output: Complete DPA template covering all Article 28 mandatory elements: processing on documented instructions only, confidentiality obligations, security measures (Article 32), sub-processor rules, data subject rights assistance, deletion/return of data, audit rights, and processor liability.
assertions: Includes all Article 28 mandatory clauses
Addresses sub-processor requirements and prior written consent
Covers data security obligations referencing Article 32
Includes data subject rights assistance clause
Addresses return or deletion of data upon termination

evals.prompt: A user has submitted a Subject Access Request (SAR). We have 30 days to respond. What exactly must our response include, what can we redact or withhold, and what are the consequences of missing the deadline?
evals.expected_output: Complete SAR response guidance: what to include (all personal data, processing purposes, recipients, retention periods, rights information), grounds for withholding (third-party data, legal professional privilege, manifestly unfounded), extension provisions, and consequences of non-compliance.
assertions: States the 30-day response deadline and 2-month extension provision
Lists all required SAR response elements per Article 15
Explains grounds for refusing or redacting (third-party data, legal privilege)
Addresses the exemption for manifestly unfounded/excessive requests
Mentions consequences: complaint to supervisory authority, potential fines

evals.prompt: We want to use analytics cookies and marketing pixels on our website for users in the EU. What consent requirements apply? Is there a legitimate interests basis we can rely on instead of consent?
evals.expected_output: Clear guidance on cookie consent requirements under GDPR and ePrivacy Directive, why marketing/analytics cookies require explicit consent (not legitimate interests), requirements for valid consent (freely given, specific, informed, unambiguous, withdrawable), and practical implementation guidance.
assertions: Explains that marketing and analytics cookies require consent, not legitimate interests
References both GDPR and the ePrivacy Directive (Cookie Law)
Describes valid consent requirements: freely given, specific, informed, unambiguous
Addresses cookie withdrawal mechanisms
Mentions that pre-ticked boxes or continued browsing do not constitute valid consent

evals.prompt: We discovered a data breach — an employee accidentally emailed a spreadsheet containing names, emails, and account numbers of 500 customers to the wrong recipient. Walk us through the GDPR breach notification requirements and what we need to do in the next 72 hours.
evals.expected_output: Step-by-step 72-hour breach response plan covering: initial assessment and containment, determining if notification is required (to supervisory authority and/or data subjects), what to include in the supervisory authority notification, when data subject notification is required, and documentation requirements.
assertions: References the 72-hour notification deadline to supervisory authority (Article 33)
Explains the threshold for when notification is required (risk to rights and freedoms)
Lists required elements of the Article 33 supervisory authority notification
Explains when data subjects must also be notified (Article 34 - high risk)
Addresses record-keeping requirements even when notification is not required

evals.prompt: We are building a healthcare app that allows patients to view their medical records and communicate with doctors. Do we need to comply with HIPAA? Are we a covered entity or a business associate?
evals.expected_output: Clear analysis of HIPAA applicability: distinction between covered entities (healthcare providers, health plans, clearinghouses) and business associates, assessment that a patient-facing app likely involves a covered entity's PHI and thus requires BAA, analysis of whether the app developer is a business associate.
assertions: Explains the three types of covered entities (healthcare providers, health plans, clearinghouses)
Explains the business associate definition and when a BAA is required
Assesses the app's HIPAA status clearly
Mentions the HITECH Act and its relationship to HIPAA
Addresses what PHI (Protected Health Information) is and the 18 PHI identifiers

evals.prompt: Draft a HIPAA Business Associate Agreement (BAA) template for a cloud storage vendor we are using to store PHI. Include all required provisions.
evals.expected_output: Complete BAA template with all required elements: permitted uses/disclosures of PHI, safeguard obligations, reporting of breaches/security incidents, access rights for covered entity, amendment and accounting requirements, return/destruction of PHI on termination, and subcontractor requirements.
assertions: Includes permitted uses and disclosures of PHI
Addresses safeguard requirements (administrative, physical, technical)
Includes breach/security incident reporting obligations
Covers termination provisions and PHI return/destruction
Addresses subcontractor/agent BAA requirements

evals.prompt: What are the HIPAA Security Rule requirements for encrypting PHI? Do we legally have to encrypt data at rest and in transit? What happens if we have a breach and the data was not encrypted?
evals.expected_output: Detailed analysis of HIPAA encryption requirements: encryption is 'addressable' (not required) under the Security Rule but practical necessity, encryption at rest vs in transit requirements, safe harbor for breach notification when data is encrypted, and consequences of unencrypted PHI breach.
assertions: Correctly states that encryption is 'addressable' not 'required' under HIPAA Security Rule
Explains the distinction between 'required' and 'addressable' implementation specifications
Addresses the breach notification safe harbor for encrypted data
Covers both at-rest and in-transit encryption considerations
Mentions HHS guidance or NIST standards for acceptable encryption

evals.prompt: Our HIPAA Security Officer needs to conduct a risk analysis. What are the required elements of a HIPAA risk analysis? What documentation do we need to produce?
evals.expected_output: Comprehensive HIPAA risk analysis requirements covering: scope (all ePHI), threat and vulnerability identification, likelihood and impact assessment, current controls evaluation, risk level determination, risk management plan, and required documentation/records.
assertions: References the HIPAA Security Rule § 164.308(a)(1) for risk analysis
Lists required elements: scope, threat identification, vulnerability identification, likelihood and impact assessment
Addresses the need to cover all ePHI across all systems
Explains documentation requirements and record retention
Mentions that risk analysis must be ongoing/periodic, not a one-time activity

evals.prompt: A nurse at our hospital accidentally accessed the medical records of a celebrity patient without a legitimate reason. What HIPAA violation occurred? What are our obligations? What penalties could apply?
evals.expected_output: Analysis of the violation (impermissible use/disclosure, Privacy Rule violation, minimum necessary standard breach), required actions (workforce sanction, breach assessment, possible HHS notification), penalty tiers for workforce violations, and practical remediation steps.
assertions: Identifies this as a Privacy Rule violation (impermissible access/use)
Mentions the minimum necessary standard violation
Addresses the requirement for workforce sanctions
Explains the breach risk assessment requirement (four-factor test)
Covers the HIPAA penalty tiers and their ranges

evals.prompt: Give me an overview of the NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0. What changed from version 1.1 and what are the 6 core functions?
evals.expected_output: Comprehensive overview of CSF 2.0: 6 core functions (Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover), what changed from 1.1 (new Govern function, scope expanded beyond critical infrastructure, new profiles and tiers guidance), and practical application guidance.
assertions: Lists all 6 CSF 2.0 functions: Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover
Highlights the new Govern function as the major addition in CSF 2.0
Explains that CSF 2.0 expanded scope beyond critical infrastructure
Mentions organizational profiles concept
Addresses the relationship between tiers and risk management maturity

evals.prompt: We are a mid-size manufacturing company that experienced a ransomware attack. Using the NIST CSF framework, help us build a 90-day recovery and improvement plan covering the Respond and Recover functions.
evals.expected_output: 90-day post-ransomware plan using CSF Respond and Recover functions: immediate containment actions, eradication steps, recovery sequence, lessons learned, and improvement priorities. Should reference specific CSF subcategories (RS.RP, RS.CO, RS.AN, RS.MI, RC.RP, RC.IM, RC.CO).
assertions: References the Respond (RS) function categories: Response Planning, Communications, Analysis, Mitigation
References the Recover (RC) function categories: Recovery Planning, Improvements, Communications
Provides a time-phased plan (immediate, short-term, 90-day)
Addresses lessons learned and improvement cycle
Includes stakeholder communication planning

evals.prompt: How do we use NIST CSF profiles to assess our current cybersecurity posture and create a target state? Walk us through the process of creating Current Profile and Target Profile.
evals.expected_output: Step-by-step guidance on creating CSF profiles: understanding organizational context and risk tolerance, mapping current controls to CSF subcategories for Current Profile, defining desired outcomes for Target Profile, gap analysis between current and target, and prioritizing improvements.
assertions: Explains the Current Profile concept (current cybersecurity outcomes)
Explains the Target Profile concept (desired/required outcomes)
Describes the gap analysis between current and target profiles
Mentions the role of business context and risk tolerance in profile creation
References the CSF tiers (1-4) as indicators of maturity

evals.prompt: Map our current controls to the NIST CSF Protect function. We have: Okta SSO with MFA, CrowdStrike EDR, Qualys vulnerability scanning, Druva cloud backup, and a written security awareness training program.
evals.expected_output: Mapping of each tool/control to the relevant CSF Protect subcategories: PR.AA (Identity Management), PR.AT (Awareness and Training), PR.DS (Data Security), PR.IR (Technology Infrastructure Resilience), PR.PS (Platform Security). Should identify gaps.
assertions: Maps Okta SSO/MFA to PR.AA (Identity Management and Access Control)
Maps security awareness training to PR.AT
Maps CrowdStrike EDR to platform security (PR.PS) or detection
Maps Qualys scanning to vulnerability management
Identifies gaps or areas not covered by current controls

evals.prompt: We are preparing for a board presentation on our cybersecurity posture. How can we use NIST CSF to communicate risk to non-technical executives? What metrics and language should we use?
evals.expected_output: Guidance on translating CSF to board-level language: using tier ratings to show maturity, framing gaps as business risks, key metrics per function, sample dashboard/reporting structure, and how to connect cybersecurity posture to business outcomes.
assertions: Explains how to use CSF tiers (1-4) to communicate maturity level to executives
Suggests translating technical gaps to business risk language
Provides metric examples for board reporting
Addresses the Govern function's role in board-level oversight
Suggests connecting cybersecurity outcomes to business objectives

evals.prompt: We are an e-commerce company that takes credit card payments on our website via Stripe. Do we need PCI DSS compliance? What SAQ level applies to us?
evals.expected_output: Clear PCI DSS applicability analysis: yes, must be compliant; explanation of merchant levels based on transaction volume; SAQ-A likely applies for Stripe redirect/iFrame implementation (no card data touches their servers); SAQ-A-EP if JavaScript on their page; guidance on scope reduction through outsourcing.
assertions: Confirms PCI DSS applies to all merchants that process payment cards
Explains the merchant level tiers (Level 1-4) based on transaction volume
Identifies SAQ-A as likely applicable for redirected payment (Stripe hosted page)
Explains cardholder data environment (CDE) scoping
Addresses how using Stripe reduces PCI scope

evals.prompt: Explain the PCI DSS v4.0 requirements for protecting stored cardholder data (Requirement 3). What changed from PCI DSS v3.2.1?
evals.expected_output: Detailed coverage of PCI DSS Requirement 3: data retention policies, prohibition on storing sensitive authentication data post-authorization, encryption requirements for PANs, key management, new v4.0 requirements (disk/partition encryption, automated processes, cryptographic architecture documentation).
assertions: States the prohibition on storing sensitive authentication data (SAD) after authorization
Addresses PAN (Primary Account Number) protection requirements
Covers encryption and key management requirements
Identifies at least 2 new or changed requirements in PCI DSS v4.0 vs 3.2.1
Mentions the March 2024 v3.2.1 retirement date (or v4.0 compliance deadline)

evals.prompt: We had a security incident where our card processing system was potentially compromised. What are our PCI DSS breach notification obligations? Who do we need to notify and in what timeframe?
evals.expected_output: PCI DSS breach notification obligations: immediate containment, notification to acquiring bank within 24 hours, PCI Forensic Investigator (PFI) engagement, card brand notification (Visa, Mastercard), potential fines, and state breach notification laws applicable to cardholder data.
assertions: Mentions immediate notification to the acquiring bank
References engagement of a PCI Forensic Investigator (PFI)
Addresses card brand (Visa/Mastercard) notification requirements
Covers the timeline obligations (typically 24-72 hours)
Mentions potential card brand fines and penalties for compromised data

evals.prompt: What are the PCI DSS requirements for penetration testing? How often must we test, what must be in scope, and what qualifications should the tester have?
evals.expected_output: PCI DSS penetration testing requirements (Requirement 11.4): annual testing plus after significant changes, both network and application layer testing required, internal and external penetration testing, segmentation testing for scoped CDE, tester qualification requirements (independence, industry-accepted methodology).
assertions: States the annual penetration testing requirement (Requirement 11.4)
Mentions requirement to test after significant infrastructure or application changes
Covers both network-layer and application-layer testing requirements
Addresses segmentation penetration testing requirements
Mentions tester independence and qualification requirements

evals.prompt: We are considering tokenization to reduce our PCI DSS scope. How does tokenization work from a PCI DSS perspective? Does it eliminate all PCI compliance requirements?
evals.expected_output: Explanation of tokenization as a scope reduction tool: how tokens replace PANs, what systems remain in scope even with tokenization (the token vault/service provider), what compliance requirements remain, limitations of tokenization (doesn't eliminate compliance entirely), and comparison to encryption.
assertions: Explains how tokenization replaces PANs with non-sensitive tokens
Clarifies that the token service provider remains in scope for PCI DSS
Addresses what compliance obligations remain after tokenization
Explains that tokenization does not eliminate all PCI requirements
Distinguishes tokenization from encryption in PCI context

evals.prompt: We operate a natural gas pipeline that falls under TSA jurisdiction. What are our current TSA cybersecurity requirements? We have heard about the security directives — summarize what they require.
evals.expected_output: Summary of TSA Pipeline Security Directives (SD-02C or current version): cyber incident reporting (24 hours), designation of cybersecurity coordinator, vulnerability assessments, implementation of TSA-recommended mitigations (network segmentation, access control, patching), annual cybersecurity incident response exercise.
assertions: References TSA Pipeline Security Directives and the current requirement framework
Mentions the 24-hour cyber incident reporting requirement to CISA
Addresses cybersecurity coordinator designation requirement
Covers the four required cybersecurity measures (access control, network segmentation, patching, monitoring)
Mentions annual cybersecurity assessment or review requirements

evals.prompt: What is a Cybersecurity Incident Response Plan (CIRP) for TSA purposes? What must it include and how often must it be tested?
evals.expected_output: Complete CIRP requirements for TSA: required plan elements (roles/responsibilities, communication procedures, containment/eradication steps, recovery procedures, coordination with government agencies), testing requirements (annual exercise), plan update requirements.
assertions: Lists the required elements of a TSA-compliant CIRP
Addresses roles and responsibilities for incident response
Covers coordination with government agencies (TSA, CISA, FBI) in incident response
States the annual testing/exercise requirement
Mentions plan review and update requirements

evals.prompt: The TSA security directives require network segmentation between OT and IT networks. We have legacy SCADA systems that were never designed to be on a segmented network. What is the practical approach to implementing segmentation for legacy OT environments?
evals.expected_output: Practical guidance on OT/IT network segmentation for legacy environments: DMZ architecture for OT/IT boundary, unidirectional gateways (data diodes) for one-way communication, jump server/bastion host approach, network monitoring considerations, compensating controls when full segmentation is not immediately feasible.
assertions: Addresses the specific challenge of legacy SCADA systems not designed for segmentation
Explains DMZ or boundary network approach for OT/IT separation
Mentions unidirectional security gateways or data diodes as an option
Addresses compensating controls where full segmentation is not immediately achievable
References TSA requirements for OT environment protection

evals.prompt: We are a small regional airport. Do TSA cybersecurity regulations apply to us? What category of aviation security does our airport fall under?
evals.expected_output: Explanation of TSA aviation security tier categories, how airports are categorized (based on passenger volume and operations), which TSA cybersecurity directives apply to airports vs airlines, and what a small regional airport's obligations are vs a major hub.
assertions: Explains that TSA cybersecurity requirements for aviation are tiered by facility category
Addresses the different obligations for airports vs airlines
Clarifies how facility categorization is determined
Identifies what a small/regional airport's likely obligations are
Mentions coordination with airport security programs and TSA Security Programs

evals.prompt: What is the difference between TSA cybersecurity requirements and CISA CIRCIA requirements? We have heard about CIRCIA — do pipeline operators need to comply with both?
evals.expected_output: Clear comparison of TSA Security Directives (sector-specific for transportation) vs CIRCIA (cross-sector cyber incident reporting law): different reporting timelines, different triggering events, relationship between the two, and whether pipeline operators must comply with both.
assertions: Explains that TSA SDs are sector-specific to transportation/pipeline operators
Explains CIRCIA as a cross-sector cyber incident reporting law
Addresses the different reporting timelines (TSA 24h vs CIRCIA 72h/24h)
Clarifies that pipeline operators may need to comply with both
Mentions CISA as the primary CIRCIA implementing agency

evals.prompt: We are a technology company deploying AI systems in healthcare decision support. What is ISO 42001 and do we need to comply with it? How does it relate to EU AI Act requirements?
evals.expected_output: Clear explanation of ISO 42001 as AI management system standard, its voluntary vs mandatory nature, key requirements (AI policy, risk assessment, impact assessment, controls), how it relates to EU AI Act (complementary, can support compliance), and why healthcare AI specifically benefits from it.
assertions: Explains ISO 42001 as an AI management system (AIMS) standard
Addresses the voluntary nature of ISO 42001 vs regulatory requirements
Covers the relationship to EU AI Act compliance
Mentions AI risk assessment and AI impact assessment requirements
Addresses healthcare AI specific considerations

evals.prompt: Walk us through the key requirements of ISO 42001 for an organization that develops AI products. What policies, processes, and documentation do we need?
evals.expected_output: Comprehensive overview of ISO 42001 requirements: AI policy, organizational roles and responsibilities, AI risk assessment process, AI system impact assessment, supplier and third-party AI controls, data quality and governance, transparency and explainability requirements, and monitoring/review processes.
assertions: Covers the AI policy requirement as a foundational element
Addresses AI risk assessment methodology requirements
Mentions AI impact assessment (AIIA) requirements
Covers data quality and data governance requirements
Addresses transparency and explainability as ISO 42001 objectives

evals.prompt: What are the unique risks that ISO 42001 addresses that are different from traditional IT risk management (like ISO 27001)? Give concrete examples of AI-specific risks.
evals.expected_output: Analysis of AI-specific risks not covered by traditional IT risk frameworks: algorithmic bias and fairness, model opacity/explainability gaps, training data quality and poisoning, model drift over time, unintended use cases, generative AI hallucinations, adversarial attacks, and supply chain risks specific to AI (pre-trained models).
assertions: Identifies algorithmic bias and fairness as an AI-specific risk
Addresses model explainability/transparency as a risk category
Covers training data risks (quality, bias, poisoning)

evals.expected_output: Explains the lex specialis relationship: DORA (Regulation (EU) 2022/2554) takes precedence over NIS2 for financial entities under Art. 4 NIS2 (sector-specific EU legal acts). evals.expected_output: Confirms the company is subject to CCPA/CPRA because it meets the $25M revenue threshold (even though it does not meet the 100K consumer threshold). Outlines core obligations: privacy notice at collection, comprehensive privacy policy, consumer rights program (know, delete, correct, opt-out, non-discrimination), service provider contracts, and opt-out mechanisms.
assertions: Confirms the company IS subject to CCPA/CPRA because it meets the $25M annual gross revenue threshold — even though it falls short of the 100,000 consumer/household threshold
Explains that meeting ANY ONE of the three thresholds (revenue, data volume, monetization) triggers CCPA/CPRA applicability
Lists core compliance obligations including privacy notice at collection, comprehensive privacy policy, and the consumer rights program (right to know, delete, correct, opt-out, non-discrimination)

evals.expected_output: Step-by-step workflow for handling a combined right-to-know and right-to-delete request: intake, identity verification (2-point match), search across all PI systems, apply exceptions (e.g., completing transactions, legal obligations, security), respond within 45 days, direct service providers to delete, confirm to consumer.

evals.expected_output: Explains that passing cookie IDs and browsing data to an ad tech platform for targeted advertising constitutes 'sharing' under CPRA (cross-context behavioral advertising) even without monetary consideration, and may also be a 'sale' depending on the arrangement. assertions: Correctly identifies that passing visitor cookie IDs and browsing data to an ad tech platform for cross-context behavioral advertising constitutes 'sharing' under CPRA even if no money changes hands

States the business must add a 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' link and must honor Global Privacy Control (GPC) signals as valid opt-outs

assertions: States that GDPR compliance provides a strong foundation (privacy notices, rights processes, processor agreements, data minimization) but does NOT automatically satisfy CCPA/CPRA
Identifies CCPA/CPRA-specific additions needed: 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' link + GPC signal compliance, and 'Limit the Use of My Sensitive Personal Information' link (CPRA)

evals.expected_output: Identifies all three data types as Sensitive Personal Information (SPI) under CPRA.
